Why, you ask, was Vermont not one of the original group of colonies that became states?

The territory between Lake Champlain and the Connecticut River was disputed ground claimed by two existing colonies, New Hampshire and New York, which the Crown ruled in NY's favor in 1764, infuriating everyone and giving rise to Ethan Allen and the Green Mountain boys, an anti-NY militia, not anti-England. 

So when independence came in 1776, Vermont had no colonial government of its own to send delegates to the Continental Congress — legally it was just a contested chunk of New York. 

In January 1777 the settlers declared themselves an independent republic (briefly "New Connecticut," then Vermont), adopted a notably progressive constitution that abolished adult slavery and established manhood suffrage without property requirements, and asked to join the union but were refused by Congress because of NY's objections. Vermont did not become a state until 1791. 

For 14 years, Vermont was an independent country, with its own currency and postal system. In 1791, Vermont resolved its issues with New York (all it took was $30,000) and became a state.

What a timely theme. We the people!  Even when those thirteen colonies banded together, it wasn't always as cool as the other side of the pillow.  Well kids, this 250th anniversary helped me recall what I forgot back in elementary school.

Take heart, fellow patriots, and lean back into the framework and the framers of this country. Most honest accounts of our early history will provide a small degree of comfort to the civic-minded citizenry. Our history shows us how this plays out.

I mean, there's always been wheeling, dealing, and stepping over the little guy. The Constitution and Bill of Rights are sacred to some, and Monopoly money to others. Huh? You know that player who lands on Free Parking and collects all the dough, but a few hotels later, when you're flush with cash and hit that corner spot, they suddenly run for the rulebook? 

Politicos have been bending the rules since the stars were just sparks. Continental currency? Trade borders and commerce? Dred Scott? Exec. Order 9066? Bonus marchers and Hoovervilles, anyone?

That being written, on most days our messy situation beats a totalitarian regime. What I believe is that greed and the pursuit of power are insignificant when you're camping by the riverbank, falling asleep to the rustling of wind through the trees. The country has survived 250 years, so let's hope it survive the ones ahead.

Is there a better example of nominative determinism than Cristóbal Colón, known to most of us here as Christopher Columbus? 

I was under the mistaken impression that he gave us the word “colony”, but no, it derives from the Latin, colonia, meaning a settlement or a farm. 

Even the Latin/Italian versions of his name, Columbus/Colombo, meaning ”dove” (or i guess “pigeon”, less romantically) suggest he was destined to fly across the ocean and bring news of some promised land.

Me loves a rebus, so what's not to love today?  Started out in the northwest corner, filled in all the letters, and said, "That was easy," except when I made up a wrong answer for 3D (PLAINANN), I had to reject it because ELN? made no sense at 36A.  Oh, yeah, I forgot.  Me loves rebuses.
Please bring back the ESC key to enter a rebus. Doesn't work no more.
Really annoying to play since the ESC key doesn't work now on my mac to toggle rebus mode.

As a non-American, I was proud that I figured out the theme to the rebuses fairly early on.  Got tripped up in some of the trivia, particularly the location of the Sloop John B - tried to figure out how to work Nassau into the grid until I realized I was overthinking it

Great puzzle!  I think it deserves serious consideration for Puzzle of the Year!
Thirteen rebuses!  And several doubles!
And then to double up the theme with American Independence related crosses!
Really brilliant!  (And I don't offer such praise lightly.)

Could it have been harder?  Sure.
Could it have been easier?  Sure.
But, to me, when we get numerous complaints on both sides of the issue, it means were in the Goldilocks Zone:
"This one is just right!!!"

Thanks David J. Kahn, for a sterling salute to American Independence!
As a non-native speaker this was a bit more tricky but I enjoyed discovering the trick with the state abbreviations. For the bottom right corner I had to look some of the proper names.

Wowza MAN! I loved this puzzle so so so much, and history is not my strong suit. I genuinely thought that I might not finish, but once I caught the theme it helped immensely. So so so fun and so many clever and innovative clues. Congrats to the constructor for a doozy of a Thursday!!

Got the rebus and the 13 colony theme when 9D was too short for SA[GA]. Thanks to high school chorus and 7th grade geography I can sing all of the states in alphabetical order and name all of the capitals. I took it further and memorized all of the postal abbreviations, as well. Fantastic puzzle for this nerd.
Fun puzzle except the ridiculous natick with Neil Diamond and Oleta Adams.   That was 5 minutes right there. MissM didn't help.
The bottom right was WAY hard. (MISS M, OLETA). This is almost not right for a Thursday puzzle (also considering the difficulty of the rebuses).
Nope.

The non theme "no knows" (looking at you SABOTS) was the challenge for me, including the groan inducing SIDEONE (well played).  

Had to run the colonies from south to north to figure out which one I was missing at the end, though.  Great puzzle, thanks.

Oh, bravo, Mr. Kahn!  My first trip through the clues yielded a lot of confusion — until the penny dropped.  Very, very clever!

As a respectful aside, if you’re tempted (or if you succumbed to the temptation) to drop a “I hate rebuses” screed here, please remember that some of us love them.  As our patron saint Deb says, “Your mileage may vary.”   It’s fine if you do you, just don’t expect the whole community to do you, and don’t belittle the creators for their construction choices.

Rant, such as it is, over.  Be kind, stay cool, and celebrate a society where (I think) we can still disagree respectfully.
Excellent puzzle! 🇺🇸

Wow I found that unnecessarily hard clueing for an already tricky Thursday rebus. I do like a rebus but got a bit frustrated at the end. I guess not allowing myself any lookups is a self imposed form of punishment when it comes to Naticks so I can't complain too much Lol
Best Rebus puzzle ever!
Today I feel dumb...
this crossword was hell, please never do something like this again (english is my second language)

This puzzle sucked. Luckily i’m from the US and i learned the colonies a zillion times in school, so that part i knew. but the fill was garbage. i understand the puzzle is constructed very ‘skillfully,’ so hats off and everything, but the puzzle was just frustrating and uninspiring to actually *play* - filled with loads of obscure names etc crossing each other.

SABOTS crosses with BO[SC]S to make the U[SC]ENSUS;  SKED with a K but CABALA with a C (and ECG with a C which is a tossup) a ‘conglomerate’ being an OLIO shoehorned in to force I[VA][NH]OE, ‘A _____’ being naturally a clue for PRIO[RI], no indication that roman numerals are used for [MD]CCC – completely lucky guess – etc etc etc 

Give me a fricking break. Sometimes a hard puzzle feels rewarding. 45 minutes of BS and a couple ‘check puzzles’ in on this one and i was just groaning. can’t remember hating a puzzle this much in ages.

very skillful creation, very unpleasant and unsatisfying puzzle. just because you can, doesnt mean you should.
Sorry, but this one is just stupid.  Random nonsense does not make a good puzzle.

I totally loved the answer to the very misleading 6-down. Usually on Thursdays, I run through the clues and put in my "known good" answers first, then try to work out the rest of the puzzle on second and third passes.

Well, today, I absolutely "knew" 6-down was "Nassau", because it says it right there in the song. But none of the acrosses would fit. Once I conceded that perhaps NASSAU was wrong, I was able to work out all of the crossings and to find SI(DE)ONE. 

Also loved the Roman numeral clue at 42A. Very tricky and fun.  My 'aha' moment came with JOH(NH)A(NC)OCK, which was as clever as the day is long! 

Brilliant!  Thank you to David J. Kahn for a very, very fun solve this morning!  Mr. Kahn... I wish for you that both sides of your pillow be cool.
Loved this puzzle! SO glad there were no circles! Amazing construction!

Let's hear it for Button Gwinnett!!  
And on the right date, too, I'm pleased to say. 
 So many appropriate Theme entries--and if not in the grid, then in the clues.  
As desperately worried about The Constitution as I am (especially given the abysmal Less-than-Supreme Court) I welcome reminders of the momentous events taking place 250 years ago.
I'm a bit slow this morning, but already 379 Comments??
Wow.
Thank you, David J. Kahn.
Wow, tough one, and really admirable construction. First time I really enjoyed (and got stuck on) a Thursday in months.

Why is it whenever a clue like  ____ island comes up my first answer is Epstein? Staten is so far down the list these days. Ach, Schadenfreude! The zeitgeist has me!
This was a lazy way of constructing a crossword.  If you can't fit a word in its entirety in the spaces available, revise your word choices.  Anyone can shorten words to fit them in the spaces.

I enjoyed it despite not being American, however I put all the correct letters in and the puzzle didn’t solve, even when I used auto check. Eventually I hit reveal all and it completed with a blue star. Very disappointing in that regard
I just adored this fun and challenging tribute to American history! 🇺🇸
I thought this was a great puzzle! Something all new with a terrific theme. I knew answers weren't fitting so I sought out the hint and it all fell into place, The rest was easy, and fun!
felt way too british for this crossword unfortunately!

Loved the puzzle with its tie-ins to US history. Go Team USA! 🇺🇸

Simply phenomenal. All 13 colonies, and all of them hidden within (horizontal) thematic entries, while also working correctly vertically. 

Only the OLETA/SHILO near-Natick detracted (just a tiny bit) from this masterful construction. Never heard of the Neil Diamond song, and couldn't recall the soul singer's name, so I had to guess there. In the end, L seemed the best choice, so I solved this one without assistance. Not at all quickly---took me just about an hour.

Felt like a truly historic puzzle today, in more ways than one. Definitely a contender for puzzle of the century.

The "check square" feature is buggy. It approves of a single letter when a rebus would be required. I had tentative single letters in a few squares but could not correct them to a rebus after checking them.  My "approved" solution shows PTRIOTS and PULA. Those Ps should be the rebus PA.
Wow was that hard! I perhaps mistakenly didn't try to keep track of which of the first states I'd surfaced yet, so finding the rebus entries was a challenge all the way through. What a workout!
Tour-de-force rebus!  I figured out the theme at PAULA Poundstone, but there was still a lot of work to be done even knowing it.

Whew. And Wow! Long workout for me, but just ended up being a fascinating puzzle. Can't imagine what it took to work those 13 rebuses into a puzzle, and... with each of them in an answer related to the theme. Again.. Wow! 

And of course a puzzle find today - I'll put that in a reply. 

....

Discovered today that the ESC button on my desktop keyboard wouldn't allow me to put more than one letter in a square.  I had to switch over to my iPhone where I could click MORE and then REBUS to fill in the rebus squares.  I realized later that I could have clicked REBUS at the top of the puzzle and filled them in that way, but I didn't think of that at the time.  This is a fairly new HP computer I bought last September.  I don't remember any rebuses on the NYT crosswords since then (I do them every day) but surely there have been some over nine months!  Is my keyboard wonky or is this a glitch in the app itself??
I might be a novice crossword solver, I might be someone from across the great pond, I might be a tad hungover, but this was breaking my brain more than it should have.
Terrific puzzle! Perfectly Thursday and perfectly timely.

I'm still working on this one, about 80% done.  The whole 13 colonies thing was rewarding and fun (I'm only missing NY and NJ as of right now).  Some of the clues though are just kind of mean.

Like "Position of Sloop John B on Pet Sounds".  Now I've been listening to Pet Sounds since my father introduced it to me 25 years ago.  On CD.  Like everyone under 60 does.  So how the heck was I supposed to know where exactly SIDE ONE ended and side two begins when nobody my age has ever owned it on vinyl?  (And people younger than me haven't even owned it on CD.)  Then who the heck shortens Schedule to SKED?

They really went out of their way to make a lot of their answers obscure and punishing today.
Excellent, fun, interesting puzzle. I wish every Thursday could be like this. Got stuck at sloop John b. BIG smile when I GOT IT.

"scattered across the grid in no discernible pattern...each of the 13 rebuses is a different pair of letters"  I see that we're just doing Connections now. If there's no discernable internal logic, we're just throwing spaghetti on the wall.

Genius!  Brilliant!  I doubt that there will be a puzzle this year that I like better than this one.  Crunchy and clever and dense and exceedingly timely.  Thank you, David and NYT editors for not taking away the challenge by telling us where the many, many rebus squares go.  Figuring that out is what makes the puzzle so hard.

I saw the trick at PATRIOTS/PRIORI/PAULA (very helpful to have known PAULA.)  But my work had only just begun.  The hardest rebus for me was the double one at JOHN HANCOCK.  Is this brilliant or what?  Embedding not one but two states in a Founding Father!  Embedding not one but two states in the heroes of the American Revolution!  And then...

Embedding NY in the bygone COLONY.  Getting NJ into the BENJAMIN and clueing it with ten HAMILTONS.  Everything belongs and is relevant to our 250th.  An incredible job of construction and an immensely entertaining and absorbing solving experience.  This will almost certainly be my choice for POY.  

David Kahn, btw, has been around forever.  Like fine wine, he just gets better with age.

Ouch!  That killed my time average...I'm happy I made it out alive.

Plus, Connections (nasty!) and Strands (a true spelling bee) were complete bears today.

I need a vacation!

BUT FOR THE LOVE OF DOG… please fix the iPad app. I must have had 40 crashes today, in spite of updating the app with the latest that claims to solve the crash issue.

Question for the group. On a computer, did the ESC key stop bringing up a rebus? Moving the mouse to click the word rebus is killing my times!

As of the last 2 or so years, one of the strongest possible signals that a good puzzle has been published (particularly during the second half of the week) is the presence of numerous comments calling the puzzle bad, griping about excessive trivia, and, if it’s got a theme that feels the least bit Thursday-ish and isn’t spoon-fed to the solver with “enter rebus here” overlays, describing the constructor as “lazy”.

Today’s verdict: greqt little Thursday!

First I want to mention that after completing the puzzle to the satisfaction of the software (on my desktop), seeing the gold star — and then reviewing it — twice now, the timer continues to tick each time despite displaying the gold star again when it is closed. The frequency of programming bugs — or intentional programming traps — is very dismaying.

This was a masterful thematic puzzle, with perhaps the only issue for me being the crossing of SHILO and ODETA, because I knew neither of them: a textbook natick.

I was willing to give this puzzle a fair shake until MDCCC for “last year of John Adams’ presidency.”

So chock full of trivia and god-awful 3-letter drivel to do… what? Shoehorn in 13 state names? Terrible, terrible, terrible. No fun wordplay, no “aha!” moments, just a very transparently obvious theme that obnoxiously impedes a bevy of “meh”-inducing answers.

I do the NYT crossword for fun and interesting wordplay, which was unfortunately entirely absent from this dull puzzle.

I don’t like to criticize crosswords too unfairly or harshly, but this puzzle was one I genuinely wish I could undo and spare myself the time and headache.

I was about a third of the way down on my first pass, without realising the theme, or the date, and thought "Gee, it's a bit American". Not long after that I realised there was a theme going on. 
I gave myself permission to not finish this one but, as it turned out, I did!
I was definitely guessing a lot of the state abbreviations, but I've watched Gary Gulman's bit enough times that I'm sure it helped.

It took me a while, but eventually I solved it.  About halfway through I realized I'd need rebuses.  A little further along, I realized they would represent the thirteen original states. One of my all-time favorites. It took just long enough to see what was going on, and it was then great fun to find the theme answers. Marvelous!
John Adams declared July 2nd would be celebrated by future generations. Alas, it’s only my birthday.

I understood the puzzle theme and got all the answers, but it would be helpful to know the correct order of letters required in the rebus squares.  All of my rebus entries were marked as errors when I checked, apparently because they weren't in the right order? It wasn't worth the time to try to figure out and correct.

Cool theme and rebus, but a few clues that really bugged me on this one.

- 11D “Hot dog on a roll?” -> SHOWOFF. I get that the author wanted a hot dog reference for the 4th of July, but this one’s a stretch.
- 48D “Doubled, say” -> GOT A HIT. In the context of baseball, I’ve never heard “double” used as a verb to describe a player hitting a double. People say, “Aaron Judge hit a double,” not “Aaron Judge doubled.”

Not really satisfied with the explanation for those ones.

- 47D implies a singular “treat,” which should be CANNOLO, but the answer was CANNOLI, which is the plural form. Tense mismatch.

An otherwise good puzzle, but those 3 clues really brought it down. I made it through eventually, but I was pretty frustrated, especially with 47D and 48D.

I’m considering this one solved, though I had to look up the list of the original 13 colonies because VT and ME were not working. I had thought of IMDB, but it didn’t click. I also had to double-check what 1800 would be in Roman numerals. This one was a chewy but fun tribute to our country. Let’s hope we have at least 250 more birthdays to celebrate … if we make it through this one (I never know these days).

This puzzle was aMAzing. Yes, some of the trivia clues were rough; it took me longer than my Thursday average, but I enjoyed every minute of it. Brilliant construction and a lot of fun.

Good fun for me (PhD in US Hist) but maybe could've waited for Saturday? And what happened to ESC as rebus key?  Maybe it's my keyboard, but it didn't work for me today.

A perfect puzzle in my view. My criteria: (1) Rebus on a Thursday that was not just a slog, and not too difficult, but tricky and kinda fun and tying into a larger theme; (2) challenging but not overwhelming; and (3) ties into a major historical event for us Yanks (recognizing that 250 is just a fraction of history for the rest of the world's countries). Thank you!

I love a rebus! It’s always a pleasant surprise to realize they’re in a puzzle. The joy of a crossword is in the uncovering. I’m surprised at the amount of people posting so much anger on a NYT crosswords comment thread lol

Explain the “printing size” —> PICA

This was difficult for sure. But the theme was so enticing I just had to roll with it.   

Since I’m not one to care how long a delightful process takes, the result was a very satisfying solve.

Imagine, if you will, an ice cream cone that lasts for over an hour!

Have you noticed this recent phenomena on the NYT where right after you read an article, you go to comments and the first thing you see is the author summarizing the article and asking for opinions?

I’m wondering whether after analyzing all the mined data information including keystrokes, the NYT has figured out that people are skipping the articles and jumping to comment? I did that yesterday on Wednesday’s puzzle, myself! Almost forgot to read Sam Corbin’s Wordplay and delighted in the photo of a spread-out Cat. How low can we go?

The journalists are now spoon-feeding us their articles.

Absolutely loved it! And also found out my Rebus key (ESC) was not working. Muscle memory was killin' my time! I have to find out how to fix that. 

However, this was a real gem, discounting that.  More please, David! 

Also adding my what the heck about the mobile app shoving the XWord off of the top of the menu. Big nope from me!

Oh ho ho, I liked this a lot!! I give it a big hip hip-huramba and a huzzah!! It is also one that, to me, proves the worth of having proper names and whatnot. They aren't my favorite part of puzzles, but they certainly serve a good purpose.... Once I got to the comic Poundstone, there was no doubt in my mind what that answer was, which allowed the nickel to drop for me. After that, it was pretty smooth sailing. Granted, if I didn't know her name, I would have had to find a different place to get that first foothold, but I have found this to often be the case. When I'm uncertain about other things that are wordplay or ambiguous clues, which I absolutely adore, knowing a good proper noun with certainty is a huge help. Enough about that, it's an argument I don't usually get involved in, but it really made a big difference to me today. Anyhow, I just thought this one was really fun and really well done. There were a number of things I didn't know, like SABOTS but I personally didn't find any unfair crossings and everything fell into place. I know not everyone is going to agree, but these are the kind of puzzles that really do give me that hip hip-huramba feeling that is ever so satisfying!! Although, I do have one nit... They got 64 cross wrong. https://youtu.be/8G3wsusVkUM?is=ywKHnRhhNr2l0-A8 Har!!

Thank you, Mr. Kahn, for this wonderful puzzle!

Yowza! What a puzzle! First, I love that there were no rebi indicators. Second, a confession: I've been saying there should be some puzzles not everyone can do, then realized I might be tolling my own death knell. Because my streak exists entirely within the Great Easening.

Well, my streak hangs by an asterisk. Almost 5x my usual length because I kept miscounting the colonies I already had. Mentally walked from GA northward counting. Still kept coming up one short. Didn't they all touch the ocean? Or was VT one?

@Francis
Back when the Staten Island ferry was a nickel, I went on a double date which we ended with a trip on the ferry. We had a quarter  which we knew was enough to cover the fare for four back to Manhattan. But none of us thought of the NYC subway fare. (We were young AND dumb.) We walked halfway up Manhattan through the dawn's early light to get to a friend's place where we could borrow the NYC fare.

@SBK in TO

😆  New York is a city of adventure.  

I loved every trip I've made there, even in the mid to late 80s when it was at a nadir.

I'd think there's a good chance I'd have lived there if I could have afforded it.

@Francis
My first permanent teaching job was in Staten Island, a borough where I'd never been. I had to commute from Brooklyn, and the fairly new Verrazzano bridge made that possible. The school was far into the borough, so I had to make a long drive past house after house that had either American flags or Republican candidate posters on their front lawns. This was during the Nixon era. I nervously wondered what the heck I was getting into. I think even today that part of NYC is still a MAGA outpost.

During the Revolution, however, Ethan Allen and the Green Mountain Boys switched enemies.

With the news of Lexington and Concord in early 1775, Allen and the Green Mountain Boys pivoted almost overnight from harassing New York surveyors to attacking the British. In May 1775, Allen (with Benedict Arnold) led the famous dawn capture of Fort Ticonderoga — one of the first offensive American actions of the war. Allen supposedly demanded its surrender "in the name of the Great Jehovah and the Continental Congress," but early witnesses report that what Allen said would not pass emu-muster in a NYT puzzle comment.

@john ezra

That is really interesting.  I'd never heard those stories before.

Do you happen to know what the population of Vermont was when it entered the Union?  I couldn't have been more than a few thousand, I wouldn't think.

Also it sounds like Ethan Allen and his Green Mountain Boys just liked fightin', regardless of who.

@john ezra

Vermont was not an original colony because the founding fathers wisely realized that VT would have been an impossible rebus in future crosswords. That’s just how far-seeing they were.

I got “Hamilton, to Geo. Washington, once”
PVTSECRETARY

I’ve got “What the US constitution created”
FEDERALGOVT

And I’ve got IVTHERAPY and TVTRAY and LIVTYLER for crosses, not too much else decent.

I made myself a list of what I thought were probably the 13 colonies (it's been a LONG time since elementary school and I've always lived in the west so I regularly forget my east coast geography). I had VT on the list, along with ME. Ended up with 14 - I was also forgetting MD - and had to look it up. 

I imagine there's a good story for Maine as well.

@SP,
Brilliant!! I see a Thursday Vermont-themed rebus xword in your future!

Much respect from,
One whose original colony/state eventually disentangled from MA (having NH between us would never have worked). 

PS  I also think this was the founding fathers plan for establishing the future of Red Sox Nation (and to keep us as far away as possible from Yankee Stadium).

I ❤️ this comment. 😂

@john ezra - Yep, a pattern in New England history. 

Vermont kicked New York out in 1777, and New York ahas been  buying Vermont back, houselot by houselot, ever since. 

Similarly, Massachusetts used to be part of Maine* -- but we kicked MA out in 1820. MA has been buying us back, houslot by houselot, ever since. 

* For the annoying pedants: Yes, we all know that ME was part of MA. But it's a JOKE... You know, HUMOR (or, maybe you don't; hence, the need for this footnote...)

@Andrzej

There is some serious idiomatic usage here.

One use of the term "hot dog" describes an exhibitionist, one who enjoys attracting attention for his behavior.  Another term for this type of person is SHOWOFF.

The phrase "on a roll" describes a hot streak, run of luck, or a series of successes.

So a "hot dog on a roll" is not a different idiom to eat but  a person seeking and gaining attention.  In other words a SHOWOFF achieving his objective.

An open mic is a venue, usually a bar or nightclub, where anyone may step up to the microphone and perform in front of an audience.  Usually they are restricted to a particular discipline such as comedy or music but I imagine there are open mic nights for POETs as well.
